risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After baseline evaluation (T0), infants were randomly assigned to either CareToy group or Standard Care group. Twins were allocated to the same group." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomly assigned, but it does not describe the random sequence generation method (e.g. computer-generated list, random numbers, blocks, stratification, or other truly random process). Under the stated criteria, "randomly assigned" alone with no additional context is insufficient to judge the sequence as truly random, so risk remains unclear.

Summary: The paper states that after baseline assessment "infants were randomly assigned to either CareToy group or Standard Care group," but it does not describe any method to conceal allocation from the enrolling personnel (e.g., central randomization, opaque sealed envelopes, or similar). Comment: Because the recruitment/enrolment staff could potentially have foreseen the next assignment from the information provided, allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Detailed information about the CareToy Project were provided to attending families." and "After baseline evaluation (T0), infants were randomly assigned to either CareToy group or Standard Care group." Comment: The trial was evaluator-blinded, but the report does not state that participants or the personnel delivering CareToy/standard care were blinded. Given the home-based, highly customized intervention, knowledge of allocation was almost certainly apparent to families and treating staff, and the outcomes (motor scales and visual acuity) could plausibly be influenced by this knowledge, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "All assessments were recorded on video and scored off-line by expert therapists, blinded to group allocation." Comment: The outcome assessors were explicitly blinded. The main motor outcomes (IMP and AIMS) were video-recorded and scored off-line by blinded expert therapists, and Teller acuity was also part of the assessed outcomes in this evaluator-blinded trial, making detection bias unlikely because allocation knowledge would not be expected to influence these measurements.

Quote: "All infants underwent 4 weeks of intervention according to their allocation... All infants were reassessed the week after the end of intervention period (T1)." Comment: The primary RCT endpoint had complete follow-up for all randomized participants, with no missing outcome data at T1. The later crossover/open-phase assessments had a small amount of attrition (18/19 and 17/22 re-evaluated at T2), but this does not appear to affect the primary randomized comparison. Overall, attrition was minimal and not suggestive of bias.
